coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots on stainless-steel nanotubes for enhanced photocatalytic degradation of phenanthrene under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Lee, Hanuk
Anwer, Hassan
Park, Jae-Woo
coredata.dc:description:
A novel nanocomposite of stainless-steel nanotubes with graphene quantum dots (SSNT@GQD) was synthesized to degrade phenanthrene photocatalytically under visible light. Photocatalytic performance of bare stainless-steel nanotubes (SSNT) is not satisfactory due to the fast recombination of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. This phenomenon was effectively overcome by inclusion of GQDs and addition of persulfate as an external electron acceptor to improve charge separation. The pseudo-first-order rate constant of phenanthrene degradation by SSNT@GQD with persulfate under visible light was 0.0211 ± 0.0006 min−1, about 42 times higher than that of persulfate and visible light, 0.0005 ± 0.0000 min−1. Effects of different water quality parameters were investigated, including levels of initial pH, natural organic matters, bicarbonate, and chloride. Sulfate radicals, superoxide radicals, and photo-generated holes were the key reactive species in this photocatalytic process. Based on the analysis of intermediates using purge and trap-GC-MS, possible photocatalytic degradation pathways of phenanthrene in this process were proposed. The SSNT@GQD showed high figure of merit (99.5 without persulfate and 78.7 with persulfate) and quantum yield (
1.56
×
10
− 5
molecules photon
− 1
without persulfate and
4.64
×
10
− 5
molecules photon
− 1
with persulfate), indicating that this material has excellent potential for practical photocatalysis applications.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots on stainless-steel nanotubes for enhanced photocatalytic degradation of phenanthrene under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Hanyang University, 222 Wangsimni-ro, Seongdong-gu, Seoul, 04763, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A novel nanocomposite of stainless-steel nanotubes with graphene quantum dots (SSNT@GQD) was synthesized to degrade phenanthrene photocatalytically under visible light. Photocatalytic performance of bare stainless-steel nanotubes (SSNT) is not satisfactory due to the fast recombination of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. This phenomenon was effectively overcome by inclusion of GQDs and addition of persulfate as an external electron acceptor to improve charge separation. The pseudo-first-order rate constant of phenanthrene degradation by SSNT@GQD with persulfate under visible light was 0.0211 ± 0.0006 min−1, about 42 times higher than that of persulfate and visible light, 0.0005 ± 0.0000 min−1. Effects of different water quality parameters were investigated, including levels of initial pH, natural organic matters, bicarbonate, and chloride. Sulfate radicals, superoxide radicals, and photo-generated holes were the key reactive species in this photocatalytic process. Based on the analysis of intermediates using purge and trap-GC-MS, possible photocatalytic degradation pathways of phenanthrene in this process were proposed. The SSNT@GQD showed high figure of merit (99.5 without persulfate and 78.7 with persulfate) and quantum yield (
1.56
×
10
− 5
molecules photon
− 1
without persulfate and
4.64
×
10
− 5
molecules photon
− 1
with persulfate), indicating that this material has excellent potential for practical photocatalysis applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) are well-known persistent organic pollutants (POPs) originating from both natural and anthropogenic sources (Jia et al., 2013; Moody et al., 2001). Due to their potential human and ecological health effects, 16 PAHs are regulated as priority pollutants by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) (Kou et al., 2008; Yu et al., 2018). Adsorption, biodegradation, light irradiation, and electrochemical oxidation are among the methods used to treat PAH-contaminated water (Shanker et al., 2017; Wigger et al., 2017; Lee et al., 2017; Pantsyrnaya et al., 2012). In the last decade, heterogeneous photocatalysis has attracted particular attention due to its utilization of clean solar energy and the absence of chemicals in the process (Chan et al., 2003; Mohapatra and Parida, 2006; Zhang et al., 2010). Titanium dioxide (TiO2) is the most widely used photocatalyst due to its environment friendliness, superior photocatalytic activity, high thermal and chemical stability, and nontoxicity (Ambrus et al., 2008; Tang et al., 2017). However, due to serious drawback as wide bandgap (∼3.2 eV), TiO2 can only restrain its use to ultraviolet (UV) range, which accounts for only about 5% of solar radiations (Quiñones et al., 2015; Yunus et al., 2017).

Many researches have recently focused attention on heterojunctions and one-dimensional nanostructures to develop the visible-light active photocatalysts (Anwer and Park, 2018; Jang and Park, 2014; Regonini et al., 2013; Zhao et al., 2016). Heterojunctions can produce the behavior of photoinduced charges in terms of the rapid charge transfer, the longer lifetime, and the reduce of recombination rate (Anwer and Park, 2018; Zhao et al., 2016). This can lead to efficient separation of photogenerated charges and thus greatly improve the photocatalytic activity. One-dimensional nanostructures (e.g., nanotubes, nanowires) offer large surface areas and provide direct pathways for photoexcited electron transfer, which enhance light absorption and carrier separation (Jang and Park, 2014; Regonini et al., 2013). Various approaches were reported to inhibit electron-hole recombination, including coupling with carbon materials (Nguyen-Phan et al., 2011; Singh and Khare, 2015), incorporation of oxidants as electron acceptors (Hu et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2017a, 2017b), acid treatment (Velegraki and Mantzavinos, 2008), and surface modification using non-metals, such as N, S, and B (Quiñones et al., 2015; Yunus et al., 2017), or metals, such as Ag, Fe, and Sr (Chen et al., 2016; Fu et al., 2018).

Stainless-steel nanotubes (SSNT) fabricated by electrochemical anodization are promising, cost-effective heterojunction materials for photocatalytic applications (Lee and Park, 2018). The SSNT surface is a p–n heterojunction of iron oxide (Fe2O3) and chromium oxide (Cr2O3), which is arranged by aligned nanopore arrays (Chamberlin et al., 2013; Chambers et al., 2005). This can lead to the efficient separation of photogenerated carriers and improved photocatalytic activity. The narrow band-gap energy of Fe2O3 (2.2 eV) aids activation at various wavelengths from the ultraviolet to the visible regions (Zhang et al., 2010). However, bare SSNT does not exhibit sufficient photocatalytic performance due to a rapid electron-hole recombination rate upon photoexcitation.

Another avenue for improving photocatalysis that has attracted significant attention is the use composites of photocatalysts with carbon materials, such as carbon nanotubes, carbon fiber, and graphene (Gu et al., 2017; Ma et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2016). These systems improve electron transport, lower the band-gap energy, increase visible-light absorption, and facilitate electron–hole separation. Of particular interest are graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which have extended π-electron systems that can be put in direct contact with the photocatalyst surface, facilitating donor–acceptor contact (Bak et al., 2016). It has been shown that the rate constant resulting from coupling TiO2 with GQDs was 1.9 times higher than that from pure TiO2 (Chinnusamy et al., 2018). In one study, more than 80% of methylene blue was removed using electrophoretic deposition-GQDs/TiO2 nanotube arrays, compared with 37% removal with TiO2 nanotube arrays alone (Li et al., 2015). In another study, more than 90% of the total ammonia nitrogen was removed using GQDs/g-C3N4, which is approximately three times more than the removal achieved with pure g-C3N4 (Wang et al., 2017a, 2017b). Similar results of improved charge-separation and photocatalytic activity are anticipated for coupling SSNT with GQDs (SSNT@GQD).

Another important and feasible strategy for reducing the probability of charge carrier recombination is addition of oxidants as electron acceptors, such as hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), persulfate (S2O8 2), peroxymonosulfate (SO5 2−), and so on (Parida et al., 2007; Wang et al., 2017a, 2017b). Oxidants can immediately trap photogenerated electrons. Persulfate is an especially attractive oxidant since it is relatively inexpensive, highly soluble in water, and stable at ambient temperatures (Liu et al., 2017, 2018). Activation of persulfate using heat, UV light, metallic cations, or transition metal oxides can also lead to generation of sulfate radical (SO4
−) to provide an effective way to degrade organic compounds in water (Chen et al., 2017; Dong et al., 2017; Hou et al., 2017; Li et al., 2017). However, it has been demonstrated that visible light only weakly activates persulfate (Hori et al., 2005). Persulfate can be activated under visible light irradiation by photo-generated electrons (Gao et al., 2016; Hu et al., 2016; Shukla et al., 2010). Here, we report a photocatalyst-activated persulfate process with SSNT@GQD as an iron-based photocatalyst. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on the potential applications of an iron-based photocatalyst with persulfate under visible light irradiation. Effects of water matrix properties, such as solution pH and the concentrations of dissolved organic matter (DOM), bicarbonate (HCO3 −), and chloride ions (Cl−), on phenanthrene degradation by this SSNT@GQD photocatalyst-activated persulfate process were evaluated. The oxidation products of phenanthrene were identified, and the degradation pathway of phenanthrene in the photocatalyst-activated persulfate process was proposed. To evaluate the performance of the photocatalyst, the quantum yield (QY) and figure of merit (FOM) of the SSNT@GQD were calculated and compared with those in previous reports.

Stainless-steel 304 plates (30 mm × 30 mm × 0.5 mm) were used as the basic material. SSNT was fabricated by electrochemical anodization process based on our previous study (Lee and Park, 2018). Briefly, its surface was mechanically polished with various abrasive papers. The polished stainless-steel plate was then anodized using platinum as the cathode in an ethylene glycol-based electrolyte with 5 vol% perchloric acid. Subsequently, the obtained SSNT samples were ultrasonically cleaned in ethanol and distilled water before drying. The GQDs were prepared by directly pyrolyzing citric acid. Two grams of citric acid were placed into a 5 mL beaker and heated to 200 °C with a heating mantle; the citric acid was liquated about 5 min later. Subsequently, the color of the liquid changed from colorless to pale yellow and then to orange in 30 min. This obtained liquid was added drop by drop into 10 mg mL−1 NaOH solution under vigorous stirring. After neutralized to pH 7.0 with NaOH, an aqueous solution of GQDs was obtained. The SSNT@GQD was fabricated by impregnation-precipitation (IP) and electrophoretic deposition (EPD) at 20 V for 5 min at various concentrations of GQD electrolyte (Su and Zhitomirsky, 2013).

Morphologies and components of the anodic films was characterized with field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, FEI Nova NanoSEM 450 and energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS, Bruker Quantax 400–10). Surface chemical states were investigated using X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific) with an AlKα X-ray source, calibrated with C 1s at 284.5 eV. The depth of the films was determined by argon ion sputtering with the reference sputtering rate of 0.78 nm s−1. The excited state of the catalysts was determined using photoluminescence (Micro Confocal PL, Mono Ra 750i) at an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. The emission was monitored from 300 to 800 nm in a single scan using a He–Cd laser. The band gap energy was calculated using UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra analyzed by UV–vis spectroscopy (Lambda 650S, PerkinElmer). XRD patterns of SSNT were not shown in this study, because they are same with previous study results (Lee and Park, 2018).

All experimental were carried out in a cylindrical reactor (Φ = 100 mm, H = 25 mm). 100 mL phenanthrene solution with appropriate volumes of persulfate were added to each reactor. Four fluorescent lamps (Eiko F4T5/D, 4 W) were used as the visible light source, and the average light intensity of each lamp at the sample was 1.65 mW cm−2, as measured with a digital lux meter (HD 2102.1, Delta-OHM). Control experiments with catalyst alone and dark experiments with catalyst and persulfate were carried out under identical conditions. Phosphate buffer solution (10 mM) was used to maintain the solution pH at 7.0, and the fine pH was adjusted with 1 N H2SO4 and 1 N NaOH using a S975 pH meter (Mettler Toledo, Switzerland). Samples of 2 mL were withdrawn from the reactor and quenched immediately with methanol to stop the chemical oxidation. All experiments were performed at least in duplicate.

The concentrations of phenanthrene, sodium diatrizoate hydrate, nitrobenzene, and para-nitroaniline were measured using high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Younglin 9100) equipped with a diode array detector (DAD, Younglin 9120) and a fluorescence detector (FLD, Younglin 9120). The samples were separated using a Zorbax SB-C18 column (4.6 × 150 mm, 5 μm particle size, Agilent) at 30 °C. The mobile phase consisted of 0.05 M ammonium acetate solution (pH 4.4) and methanol in a volumetric ratio of 50:50 at 1.0 mL min−1 flow rate. Degradation products of phenanthrene were identified using purge and trap-gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (P&T-GC-MS) using an Agilent 7890A GC with 5975C series mass spectrometer equipped with an Agilent DB-5ms column (30 m × 250 μm × 0.25 μm). Helium was used as the carrier gas at a flow rate of 1.0 mL min−1 with splitless injection at 300 °C. The oven temperature was programmed as follows: 60 °C was held for 1 min, then the temperature was ramped at 5 °C min−1 to 300 °C, and finally 300 °C was held for 5 min. The scanning range was from 50 to 500 m/z. The SRFA concentrations were expressed as total organic carbon (TOC, mgC L−1) and determined using a Shimadzu TOC-L analyzer (Japan). Persulfate and H2O2 concentrations were measured by a spectrophotometric method, which is presented in Supplementary Material (Text S1). The degradation rate constants of phenanthrene with SO4
-, hydroxyl radical (OH), and carbonate radical (CO3
-) were calculated using competitive kinetic methods. A detailed description of these methods is presented in Supplementary Material (Text S2).

Fig. 1 shows surface morphologies of the SSNT and SSNT@GQD. SSNT showed a hexagonal pore structure with 75 nm diameter in Fig. 1(a). The underlying nanotubular structure was highly ordered, compactly arranged, and vertically aligned. As shown in Fig. 1(b), the pores of the SSNT@GQD prepared by the IP method were clogged with the aggregated GQDs before the GQDs entered the nanotubes, preventing full utilization of the internal areas of the SSNT to form intimate SSNT-GQD heterogeneous junctions (Li et al., 2015). On the other hand, the SSNT@GQD prepared by the EPD method showed a uniform GQD coating layer on the outer surface of the SSNT (Fig. 1(c) and (d)). Therefore, the SSNT@GQD prepared by the EPD method were used in subsequent experiments. In EDS analysis, major elements of the SSNT were Fe, O, and Cr with atomic percentages of 65.06, 16.82, and 16.29%, respectively (Fig. S2(a)). The C 1s peak was revealed in the EDS result of the SSNT@GQD, indicating that GQDs were successfully impregnated onto the SSNT surface (Fig. S2(b)). Major elements of the SSNT@GQD were similar to those of the SSNT, and the atomic percentage of C from the GQDs on the SSNT@GQD surface was 43.22%.

Fig. 2 shows the XPS depth profiles with argon ion sputtering and the XPS high-resolution spectra of the SSNT@GQD. After a sputter time of 100 s relative concentrations of all species were stable, indicating that the depth of SSNT@GQD was approximately 78 nm (Fig. 2(a)). Fig. 2(b) shows the four peaks at 284.6, 286.0, 287.5, and 288.8 eV correspond to C–C, C–O, CO, and O–CO, respectively. These values matched well with XPS spectra of GQDs reported in the literature, confirming that GQDs were successfully deposited onto the surface of the SSNT (Bak et al., 2016; Chinnusamy et al., 2018; Li et al., 2015). Fig. 2(c)∼(e) confirm the SSNT surface components that were in our previous report (Lee and Park, 2018). Fig. 2(f) shows the Ni metal main peak at 852.6 eV in inner phase, while there was no peak in outer phase due to release of Ni during anodization. Therefore, Ni may not affect to SSNT photocatalytic property.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra and Kubelka-Munk plots of the SSNT and SSNT@GQD catalysts are shown in Fig. 3 . The PL intensity was affected by the recombination rate of e− and h+ pairs. A lower PL intensity indicates an improved charge separation of e− and h+ pairs. A low PL spectra intensity was observed in the SSNT@GQD catalyst compared to SSNT alone, as in Fig. 3(a). This was due to the GQDs acting as electron transporters, which hindered e− and h+ recombination for the SSNT (Hu et al., 2015). This enhanced charge separation efficiency increased the efficiency of the photocatalytic process under visible light irradiation. The bandgap energy of the synthesized photocatalysts was obtained from the Kubelka–Munk function (Anwer and Park, 2018):

is in Kubelka−Munk units and R is the reflectance at 1000 nm set at 100%. Estimated bandgap values of the SSNT and SSNT@GQD catalysts corresponded approximately to the light responsibility with energy bandgaps of 2.28 and 2.02 eV, respectively (Fig. 3(b)). The bandgap energy of the SSNT was shifted to lower energy when coupled to GQDs, which can be attributed to the sub-band gap states between the SSNT and GQDs (bandgap less than 2.0 eV) (Quan et al., 2016; Zeng et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2012).

Phenanthrene degradation with and without visible light irradiation was monitored at 0.1 wt% GQDs (Fig. 4 (a) and (b)). Without visible light irradiation, 11.7% and 8.5% of the phenanthrene was degraded after 6 h in the presence of GQDs and SSNT@GQD, respectively, which indicated the adsorption of phenanthrene. About 14% and 17% phenanthrene degradation was observed with the persulfate alone and persulfate with SSNT@GQD over 6 h, respectively. This was due to high solution oxidation potential of the persulfate anion, E0 = 2.10 V vs. NHE (Gao et al., 2016).

Photocatalytic performance of various samples is shown in Fig. 4(b). Negligible phenanthrene decay occurred under visible light irradiation alone since phenanthrene does not undergo photolysis with visible light (Zhao et al., 2016). The sample with persulfate alone showed negligible degradation of phenanthrene under visible light irradiation as well, since persulfate is not activated by visible light (Gao et al., 2017). The presence of SSNT and SSNT@GQD led to 81% and 93% phenanthrene degradation, respectively, after 6 h of irradiation. The pseudo-first-order rate constant of phenanthrene with SSNT@GQD (0.0211 ± 0.0006 min−1) was 3.4 times higher than that with SSNT (0.0071 ± 0.0004 min−1). This was because the GQDs adsorbed more phenanthrene and were superb mediators of charge separation and transfer. When phenanthrene was treated with SSNT@GQD and persulfate, complete degradation of phenanthrene was observed after 4 h of visible light irradiation. This result was due to the formation of SO4 ·- as a strong one-electron oxidant (E0 = 2.60 V vs NHE) by the acceptance of a photo-generated electron during the photocatalytic reaction (Shukla et al., 2010).

The photocatalytic activity as a function of GQD content is shown in Fig. 4(c) and Table 1 . Incorporation of GQDs increased the photoactivity of the SSNT: each sample containing GQDs exhibited higher activity compared to SSNT alone. This is attributed to an improved charge separation of the GQD-SSNT interface. The highest activity was observed in the SSNT@0.5% GQD sample, with lower activity observed for GQD content both above and below this value. The higher GQD contents (1.0% and 5.0%) showed the reduced photocatalytic activity, because the shield of the SSNT surface by surplus GQDs resulted in retardation of the charge transfer from the catalyst to phenanthrene molecules (Wang et al., 2017a, 2017b).

The evolution of reactant concentrations during phenanthrene degradation by photocatalyst-activated persulfate with 0.5 wt% of GQD is presented in Fig. 5 (a). The initial phenanthrene concentration was 10 μM, the initial persulfate concentration was 1 mM, and the initial solution pH was 7.0. The phenanthrene concentration quickly and monotonously decreased under visible irradiation, until it was completely removed within 160 min. TOC removal was slower than phenanthrene degradation due to the formation of intermediate oxidation byproducts of phenanthrene degradation. Persulfate concentration decreased almost linearly, and H2O2 was produced with a maximal concentration of 38 μM and reached equilibrium after 160 min (Lee et al., 2014).

To evaluate the reusability of the SSNT@GQD, five repeated experiments were performed under the same photocatalyst and conditions. The SSNT@GQD maintained high photocatalytic activity over the repeated uses, as shown in Fig. 5(b). Phenanthrene was degraded completely within 160 min in all five cycles, and the leaching of metal ions (Fe, Cr, and Ni) throughout the consecutive experiments was negligible (data are not shown). These results demonstrate that the SSNT@GQD can be reused for sustainable photocatalytic oxidation processes.

Practical applications of photocatalysts require preliminary evaluations of various operating parameters that might affect photocatalytic performance. Effect of initial pH, DOM, HCO3 −, and Cl− on the degradation of phenanthrene with SSNT@GQD and persulfate under visible irradiation were investigated. Summarized results are presented in Fig. 6 and Table 1.

Phenanthrene degradation kinetics at different initial pHs of 3, 7, and 11 were monitored (Fig. 6(a)). Phenanthrene degradation was similar with increasing of pH from 3 to 7. However, oxidation declined at pH 11, as only 77% of phenanthrene was removed in 4 h. The acidic condition therefore seems more suitable for organic degradation in the persulfate process (Pu et al., 2014). More efficient elimination at acidic condition can be explained by the increase of SO4
- generation rate as pH decreases (Deng et al., 2013; Timmins et al., 1999). OH was the predominant radical under basic conditions (Timmins et al., 1999). The radical interconversion reactions between SO4
- and OH at basic pH generated inactive products, such as SO4 2−, HSO4 −, and oxygen, which reduced the concentration of active species and thus led to less degradation of phenanthrene (Lau et al., 2007).

Phenanthrene degradation was drastically inhibited by addition of Suwannee River fulvic acid (SRFA) as DOM, as shown in Fig. 6(b). DOM is well known as scavenger of reactive species such as SO4
- and OH, so it may contribute to the inhibitory mechanism on phenanthrene degradation (Eq. (2)) (Li et al., 2017). Another possibility is that the light screening effect of DOM strongly weakens the radiant flux necessary for photocatalyst activation of persulfate. In addition, DOM was reported that it can quench the cation radicals of organic contaminants (Chen et al., 2017). In this study, the phenanthrene cation radical (PHE
+) can be reduced by DOM through the (Eq. (3)).

The effect of HCO3 − was investigated at pH 8.2, where 98% of carbonate species are in the form HCO3 −. The presence of HCO3 − had a slight negative effect on photocatalyst-activated persulfate oxidation of phenanthrene, as shown in Fig. 6(c). Generally, bicarbonate showed the quenching ability of SO4

Phenanthrene degradation was inhibited at 500 mM of Cl−, as shown in Fig. 6(d). The Cl− played a complex role due to generation of less reactive chlorine species (Cl and ClOH -) through the following reactions (Liang et al., 2006; Yang et al., 2014):
(6)

Eqs (6) and (7) showed the reversible equilibrium reaction between the sinks for SO4
- and OH and backward reactions (Liang et al., 2006). At low chloride concentrations (1–100 mM), the backward reaction of Eq. (6) was dominant reaction because the SO4
- scavenging by Cl− was weak. However, high concentrations of Cl− (i.e., >300 mM) could favor the forward reaction, leading to consumption of sulfate radicals. Thus, the inhibition effect at high concentrations of Cl− could be attributed to the scavenging of SO4
- by generation of the less reactive species.

To investigate the major reactive species, radical scavenging experiments were performed using tert-butanol (t-BuOH), ethanol (EtOH), oxalic acid (OA), and benzoquinone (BQ) (Fig. 7 (a)) (Liu et al., 2017; Wang and Zhang, 2011). A slight effect on the degradation of phenanthrene was observed in the presence of t-BuOH, a scavenger of ·OH, suggesting that OH plays a minor role in the degradation of phenanthrene using SSNT@GQD and persulfate under visible irradiation. This is because the H2O2 concentration produced during this process was very low, although the second-order rate constant of phenanthrene with OH (

(13)

Fig. S3 shows the total ion chromatograms and the MS spectra of phenanthrene and its photodegradation byproducts produced during the photocatalytic reaction. Fig. 8 depicts the degradation pathways of phenanthrene in the SSNT@GQD photocatalytic process; three possible degradation pathways were proposed. First, SO4
-/OH might attack phenanthrene’s aromatic ring at the 9- and 10- positions to form 9-phenanthrol (product B) or 9,10-phenanthrenediol, because the 9,10-double bond of phenanthrene is the most electron-rich position (Woo et al., 2009). However, 9,10-phenanthrenediol was not detected in our samples as it can be easily translated to 9,10-phenanthrenequinone (product C1), which was the most common product of phenanthrene identified in other reports (Larson et al., 1988; Zhao et al., 2016). With further oxidation, CO might be removed; then, through hydroxylation and oxidation, 2′-(hydroxymethyl)[1,1′-biphenyl]-2-ol (product D) was produced. For the second pathway, SO4
-/OH attacked phenanthrene’s side aromatic rings at the 2- and 3- positions to form 2,3-phenanthrenediol, after which it was hydroxylated and transformed to 2,3-phenanthrenequinone (product C2) (Lee and Lane, 2010; Yu et al., 2018). Then the open-ring product 1-naphthaleneacetic acid (product E) would be generated by loss of oxalic acid and hydroxylation from phenanthrenequinone. For the third pathway, O2
- attacks the 10- position of phenanthrene to form the ionic intermediates, then these intermediates are dehydrated and dehydrogenated to form phenanthro [9,10-b]oxirene (product F) (Hull et al., 2010). After these three possible stages, 1-aromatic ring compounds, such as phthalic acid (product G), benzoic acid (product H), and bis(2-ethylbutyl) phthalate (product I), were derived from the further oxidation and ring-opening of aforementioned intermediates by various radicals (Fu et al., 2018; Jia et al., 2012). At the end of the photocatalytic reaction, many small organic compounds were detected, such as phenol (product J), 2-cyclohexen-1-one (product K), 2-cyclohexan-1-ol (product L), 1,2-cyclohexanedione (product M), 1,3-cyclohexadiene (product N), 2-hexen-1-al (product O), and methyl 2-hydroxybutanoate (product P), which were then mineralized to CO2 and H2O (Step 6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yield and figure of merit
ce:section.ce:para:
As energy consumption is import consideration in a photocatalyst system, the apparent QY of the photocatalytic reaction was calculated as (Kisch and Bahnemann, 2015)
(14)
QY =
Decay rate
(
molecules s
− 1
)
Photon flux
(
photons s
− 1
)

To investigate the relative performance of our system, important photocatalytic characteristics (product yield, contaminant load, catalyst mass, reaction time, and energy consumption) were considered to estimate a FOM according to (Anwer and Park, 2018). The mass of SSNT was calculated using Faraday’s law and was 0.0268 g (Jang and Park, 2014).
(15)
FOM =
Product obained
( L )
Catalysts mass
(
g
)
× Time
(
h
)
× Energy consumption
( Wh
μ mol
− 1
)

The obtained FOM values were modified by rescaling to bring them into a suitable range (0–100). Modified FOM (FOMm) values with corresponding system QYs are listed in Table 2 . To the best of our knowledge, the SSNT@GQD had a high QY both without persulfate (1.56 × 10−5 molecules photon−1) and with persulfate (4.64 × 10−5 molecules photon−1) compared to all examined systems. The FOMm values demonstrate that our system using SSNT@GQD has great utility, with a good removal and economic efficiency. Moreover, SSNT@GQD used only 12.8 and 6.4 Wh of electrical energy to degrade 1 μmol of phenanthrene without and with persulfate, respectively.

We prepared a novel SSNT@GQD photocatalyst with a uniform tubular structure and incorporation. The photocatalyst showed a strong light absorption over the UV–vis region. Heterojunction formation between Fe2O3 and Cr2O3 assisted electron–hole separation, and the GQD component improved photocatalytic performance by enhancing electron mobility. This SSNT@GQD shows great photocatalytic activity, which is comparable ability under other photocatalyst systems with similar conditions. Even after five photocatalysis cycles, phenanthrene was completely removed by the SSNT@GQD, indicating good stability and reusability for practical applications. SO4
-, OH, and O2
- were generated during the photocatalytic reaction, which accounted for the increase in photocatalyst photoactivity under visible light radiation. We also provided useful information for its practical application under various water quality conditions, degradation mechanism, and the possible pathways. Comparisons of the QYs and FOMs of various photocatalyst systems confirmed the excellent efficiency of SSNT@GQD.
